original_abstract: De testamento is a both theological and a historically biblical treatise in which Bullinger discusses themes such as, for example, justification by faith, God’s accommodation to mankind, the covenant as the subject or focus of all Scripture, the covenant with Adam, the unity of the Old and New Testaments, the seed of Abraham, the covenant and right living, the Holy Spirit and covenant, the Law and covenant, the conditions of covenant, the ceremonies of covenant and the sacrament of the covenant. Far from being a work that only focuses on the covenant De testamento reveals Bullinger as a biblical theologian who is committed to challenge the elect to live integer before the sovereign El Shaddai. An examination of the Latin terms for "covenant" (foedus, testamentum and pactum) reveals that, for Bullinger, the terms are interchangeable. However, he avoids using pactum lest pactum theology be read into his work. His use of the terms are more consistent than that of Zwingli which indicates a measure of independence from Zwingli in his understanding of the covenant.
humanized_abstract: De testamento is a treatise concerning the theological and biblical aspects of the covenant. Bullinger expounds on a number of themes here that are important to his theology, for example justification by faith, God’s accommodation to humanity, that the covenant is the theme or content of Scripture, that there is a covenant with Adam, that there is unity between the Old and New Testaments, that the seed of Abraham is Christ, that the covenant has to do with righteous living, that the Holy Spirit is the Spirit of the covenant, that the Law is the covenant, that the conditions of the covenant have to do with faith, and that the ceremonies of the covenant are related to the sacrament of the covenant. In De testamento Bullinger emerges as a biblical theologian urging the elect to live the whole way of life of the integrated man before the sovereign El Shaddai. An examination of Bullinger’s use of the Latin words for “covenant” (foedus, testamentum, and pactum) shows that to Bullinger they are interchangeable. Bullinger prefers not to use pactum in order to avoid being contaminated with the pactum theology of others. Bullinger’s use of the terms is more consistent than in the case of Zwingli, a fact which shows that Bullinger was not heavily influenced by the way in which Zwingli used these terms.
